exemplar.title: To what extent will Taylor Swiftâs Eras Tour have a positive impact on the hotel industry in Toronto in November of 2024?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent will Taylor Swiftâs Eras Tour have a positive impact on the hotel industry in Toronto in November of 2024?

subject.title: Economics
subject.description:
**IB Economics** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **microeconomics, macroeconomics, the global economy, and development economics**, with a strong focus on **analytical and evaluative skills, real-world application of economic theory, and quantitative analysis**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Extended response questions | 1h 15min | 1h 30min | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Data response questions | 1h 45min | 1h 45min | 40% (SL), 30% (HL) |
| **Paper 3** | Mathematical questions | N/A | 1h | 20% (HL only) |
| **Internal Assessment** | Portfolio of 3 commentaries | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Research question is clearly stated and tightly linked to the analysis throughout.
Topic is communicated accurately with a clear economic purpose and focus.
Methodology is generally relevant, with a plausible primary-data collection design.
weaknesses: Methodology section omits important details (distance-band justification, statistical significance tests).
Sampling approach is convenience-based with no justification of date selection.
Minor lapses in wording and occasional digressions limit full methodological rigor.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work demonstrates a clear, focused research question and communicates its purpose effectively, meriting at least a mid-band score. However, gaps in methodological completeness (unfinished sentences, lack of detail on analysis tools, convenience sampling) prevent maximum marks. A score of 4/6 appropriately rewards overall strong focus while acknowledging these limitations.

strengths: Source material is applied in a mostly relevant and appropriate way to support the investigation.
Economic concepts (oligopoly, price discrimination, externalities) are explained correctly and coherently.
Terminology and diagram labels are generally accurate and consistent.
weaknesses: Heavy reliance on general news outlets and lack of Toronto-specific academic research.
Incomplete citations (missing publication years) and undefined abbreviations (e.g., âSOâ).
Some concepts are simplified or repetitive, limiting depth of understanding.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student shows clear knowledge of core economic concepts and uses source material appropriately, fitting the middle band. However, limitations in source depth, incomplete bibliographic details, and occasional oversimplification constrain the work to a score of 3/6.

strengths: Argumentation is coherent and conclusions follow logically from the data analysis.
Primary-data research is relevant and clearly tied to the research question.
Evaluation acknowledges key limitations (timing, data confidentiality) and uses quantitative results effectively.
weaknesses: Evaluation is only partially criticalâalternative explanations and policy implications are underdeveloped.
No formal statistical testing is described, so causality claims lack support.
Confounding factors (seasonality, currency fluctuations) are noted briefly but not rigorously explored.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation demonstrates solid critical thinking: relevant research, clear analysis, and some critical evaluation. Yet the absence of deeper evaluation (no policy discussion, limited exploration of confounders) keeps it short of the top band. A mid-range score of 5/12 reflects these strengths and weaknesses.

strengths: Report structure follows IA conventions with a clear table of contents, headings, and numbering.
Figures and tables are placed logically within the text.
Word count, title page, and pagination requirements are met.
weaknesses: Several layout issues: low-resolution graphics, URLs breaking across lines, and oversized tables.
Inconsistent figure/table numbering and orphaned captions disrupt readability.
Appendix data is overwhelming and lacks summarising metrics in the body.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Overall structure and basic formatting conventions are in place, but multiple presentation flaws (resolution, layout, caption consistency) prevent a higher mark. A score of 2/4 appropriately reflects competent but imperfect presentation.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is stated verbatim in the title and again in the introduction.  It is narrow (single city, single month, specific industry) and firmly connected to the later data analysis.  Focus is maintained in the body, where all graphs, tables and discussion return to the same question.  Hence it satisfies the descriptor of a clear, focused, and effectively connected RQ.

The student communicates the topic with a clear focus on the Toronto hotel market during the Eras Tour, explains why it is economically interesting, and links it explicitly to oligopoly, price discrimination and externalities.  The purposeâto gauge the positive impact on hotel revenueâis explicit and sustained throughout the paper.  Minor lapses in wording (âchappest roomâ) and a few digressions into general tourism issues stop it from being exemplary, but overall the topic is accurately and effectively conveyed.

Primary hotel-price data and secondary articles (Washington Post, STR, academic paper by Falk & Vieru) are used in the narrative and graphs; most are relevant to the investigation.  Nonetheless, reliance on news outlets, absence of Toronto-specific academic research, and little triangulation mean application is âmostlyâ but not âclearlyâ appropriate.

The report follows the required IA sections, includes a table of contents and consistent heading system, and places figures and tables logically.  Minor issuesâoccasional orphaned captions, figures running onto new pages without referenceâprevent a perfect score but overall structure is âclearly appropriateâ.

Font, spacing and labelling are mostly compliant; word count, title and page numbers are present.  Nevertheless, several URLs break across lines, some tables overflow margins, and figure resolution is low, indicating that âsome layout considerations are incorrectâ.  Hence the lower 1-2 band is suitable, with a score of 2.

The argument is coherent and the evaluation section recognises limitations (event not yet occurred, lack of occupancy data, ceteris paribus).  However, evaluation is partial: alternative explanations (seasonality, currency effects) are barely explored and policy implications are absent.  Structure is reasoned but not deeply critical, so a 4 within the 4-6 band is justified.

The student undertakes comparative analysis (concert vs non-concert prices, distance bands) and links results to elasticity, oligopoly and externalities.  Conclusionsâthat prices spike and benefits diminish with distanceâfollow logically from the evidence.  Yet causality is asserted rather than statistically tested, confounding factors are acknowledged only briefly, and no economic surplus is quantified.  Depth is thus âclearâ¦mostly supportedâ, matching a mid-band score of 4.

The student demonstrates sound knowledge of oligopoly, price discrimination and externalities, integrating these into the discussion.  Explanations of demand inelasticity and dynamic pricing are correct but occasionally simplified or repetitive.  Overall coherence is present, yet depth is moderate rather than sophisticated, aligning with the 3-4 band.

The majority of sourcesâhotel websites for price data and economic literatureâare directly relevant to the RQ.  Primary data dominate and are clearly matched to the question.  Some sources (e.g., general tourism papers from 2002) are peripheral, and data authenticity is assumed rather than verified, so the work sits solidly in âmajority appropriate and clearly relevantâ but not at the consistently excellent level.

The student outlines a plausible primary-data method (collecting 30 hotel prices, three distance cohorts, comparing concert vs non-concert dates) and justifies choices such as using official hotel websites and identical room types.  However, sampling is convenience-based, no explanation of how specific dates were selected, and statistical tools are limited to percent change and standard deviation with no testing of significance.  Source range is adequate but not wide.  These limitations point to âgenerally relevant sources / some evidence of informed selectionâ, matching the 3-4 band.

Economic terminology (e.g., âthird-degree price discriminationâ, ânegative externality of productionâ, âstandard deviationâ) is frequent and generally accurate.  Diagrams are labelled with MSC, MPC, MR, etc., correctly.  Minor slips (âchappestâ) and a few unclear abbreviations prevent full consistency, but the work comfortably meets âaccurate and consistentâ within this middle band, so the upper score of 4 is appropriate.

annotations.comment: The definition of MSC and MPC in the negative externality section could be more precise; include formal definitions and clarify cost components.
The inequality for positive externalities is misstated. Revisit whether MSC<MPC or MPC<MSC is correct for external benefits, and adjust accordingly.
The research question is clearly stated in the title and introduction, providing precise scope and focus for the investigation.
The introduction effectively contextualises the economic significance of the Eras Tour and frames the scope by specifying data collection methods.
The background section relies heavily on general tourism sources; incorporating Toronto-specific academic research would deepen the analysis.
Figure 1âs resolution is low and the axes labels are blurred; replace with a higher-quality or vector graphic to improve readability.
The repeated use of âEssentiallyâ and lengthy sentences in the elasticity explanation make the text verbose; streamline language for clarity.
The Shangri-La citation lacks a publication year; ensure every online source includes full bibliographic details and access dates.
The methodology description ends mid-sentence and omits how secondary sources will be integrated; complete the section and clarify your approach.
Provide justification for the chosen 1âkm, 3âkm and 5âkm distance bands, explaining how these cutoffs were determined rather than by convenience.
The typo âchappestâ should be corrected to âcheapestâ to maintain academic tone and precision.
The claim to test statistical significance is unsupported without naming a test or specifying p-value thresholds; detail the planned analytical procedure.
Abbreviations like âSOâ for sold out are not defined; include a legend or footnote when first used to avoid reader confusion.
The percent-difference discussion clearly ties results to the hypothesis and demonstrates effective use of data in support of conclusions.
To control for seasonality, consider including comparative data from October or December to isolate the Tourâs unique impact.
Citation URLs break mid-line in the bibliography, disrupting readability. Wrap URLs properly or hyperlink them in a consistent style.
Ensure each online source includes an access date and consistent bibliographic formatting to meet academic referencing conventions.
Appendix Table 10 presents 30 hotel columns that overwhelm the reader; split the data into smaller tables or summarise key metrics in the body.
Using sporting events as proxies for concert impacts requires justification; discuss demographic or behavioural parallels that support this transfer.
The bar graph caption refers to âFigure 6â though this is labelled as Figure 6 in the text; ensure consistent numbering in captions and references.
Reference each figure by number in the narrative (e.g., âsee Figure 10â) to guide the reader smoothly through visual data.
Figure 12âs caption is embedded within a figure group; separate the caption into its own block to improve clarity.
The quantitative summary of price differences in evaluation is thorough, clearly linking primary data to the research question.
Consider discussing potential currency fluctuations or concurrent events (e.g., sports games) that might influence hotel demand alongside the tour.
The evaluation section sensibly acknowledges data confidentiality and timing limitations, strengthening the credibility of the analysis.
Include mean and standard deviation summaries alongside the raw appendix tables to help readers interpret the large dataset more effectively.
Appendix Table 0 contains âSOâ entries without legend; add a footnote explaining âSOâ stands for sold out to avoid ambiguity.
